questions.question: Which role advises an organisation on data protection law and acts as the contact point for the Information Commissioner's Office?
options: Data Protection Officer (DPO)
Registered care worker
Caldicott Guardian
Senior Information Risk Owner (SIRO)

questions.question: Organisations that handle NHS patient data complete which annual online self-assessment against national data security standards?
options: The Data Security and Protection Toolkit
A retention schedule
A subject access request
A privacy notice

questions.question: How many lawful bases does the UK GDPR set out for using personal data?
options: Seven
Five
Four
Six

questions.question: For special category data such as health information, the extra condition usually relied on in care is:
options: A court order
The condition for the provision of health or social care
The person's signed consent form
The legitimate interests of a visitor

questions.question: How long does an organisation normally have to respond to a subject access request?
options: One month
One year
72 hours
One week

questions.question: Under the UK GDPR, for consent to be valid it must be:
options: Impossible to withdraw once given
Given only in writing on an official form
Freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous
Assumed from a pre-ticked box

questions.question: Which body expects services to keep records securely and use information responsibly as part of being safe and well-led?
options: The Data Protection Officer
The Senior Information Risk Owner
The Care Quality Commission (CQC)
The person's next of kin

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a personal data breach?
options: Shredding old records in confidential waste
An email with sensitive details sent to the wrong person
Storing paper records in a locked cabinet
Locking your screen when you step away

questions.question: Retention periods for how long health and care records should be kept are set out in which document?
options: The Data Protection Act 2018
The Caldicott Principles
The privacy notice
The Records Management Code of Practice

questions.question: The right to rectification means that a person can:
options: Ask you to correct information that is wrong or incomplete
Insist their data is shared with anyone they choose
Ask to see all the information held about them
Demand that all their records are deleted immediately

questions.question: Which of these is classed as special category data?
options: A person's date of birth
A person's care ID number
A person's religious or philosophical beliefs
A person's home address

questions.question: A resident says, "I want to see everything you've written about me." What should you do?
options: Hand over the full care file on the spot
Refuse, because records are confidential to staff
Recognise it as a subject access request and report it to your manager promptly
Tell them they must fill in a special form first before anything happens

questions.question: Why does day-to-day care record-keeping usually NOT rely on the person's consent as its lawful basis?
options: Because consent is illegal in health and social care
Because consent can be withdrawn at any time, yet records still need to be kept accurately
Because consent lasts forever and cannot be changed
Because residents are never asked about their care

questions.question: The principle of purpose limitation requires that personal information is:
options: Only used for the specific purpose it was collected for
Collected in the largest amount possible
Shared freely with any colleague who asks
Kept forever in case it is useful later

questions.question: You should switch on multi-factor authentication for work accounts where it is offered.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is fine to save confidential information onto a personal memory stick, as long as you delete it afterwards.
options: True
False

questions.question: The right to erasure is an absolute right, so a person's records must always be deleted whenever they ask.
options: True
False

questions.question: A subject access request can be made verbally, including through social media, and does not have to use a special form or the words 'subject access request'.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of a personal data breach? (Select all that apply)
options: A care folder or handover sheet left on a bus
Records left open on a screen where a visitor can read them
Checking the recipient carefully before sending a secure email
Storing paper records in a locked cabinet
A lost, unencrypted phone or USB stick

questions.question: Which of the following are good practices when working away from the office? (Select all that apply)
options: Avoid working over unsecured public Wi-Fi
Keep records out of sight of family and members of the public
Post photographs of the people you support on social media
Only use devices and networks your employer allows
Save confidential records onto your own personal laptop

questions.question: Which of the following are senior roles that support information governance in health and social care? (Select all that apply)
options: Fire safety officer
Caldicott Guardian
Catering supervisor
Data Protection Officer (DPO)
Senior Information Risk Owner (SIRO)

questions.question: In which situations can you share information without the person's consent? (Select all that apply)
options: A legal duty, for example where a court order requires it
Vital interests, in a medical emergency to protect someone's life
To discuss the person's details with friends outside work
Because a colleague is curious about a service user
Safeguarding, where an adult or child is at risk of abuse or serious harm

questions.question: Because care records are full of ___ data, most of the information you handle is special category data and must be treated with particular care.
The Caldicott principles ask you to use the ___ amount of confidential information necessary for the task, and no more 'just in case'.
When paper records reach the end of their life they must be ___ or placed in confidential waste, never put in an ordinary bin.
